title: Tsoureki
subtitle: Greek braided Easter bread scented with mahlab and orange
excerpt: Tsoureki is the enriched bread Greeks bake at Easter: braided, golden from eggs, and fragrant with mahlab (the ground kernel of the St Lucie cherry stone) and orange zest. The dough is richer than brioche, the braid tighter, and the crumb pulls into long, thin strands when you tear it. It keeps well for three days at room temperature, wrapped.
sourceNotes: Tsoureki is the standard Greek Easter bread, made across Greece and the Greek diaspora. Recipes appear in Vefa Alexiadou, 'Greek Cuisine' (1989) and Diane Kochilas, 'The Food and Wine of Greece' (1990). Mahlab has been used in Greek and Levantine baking since at least the Ottoman period, documented in nineteenth-century domestic guides from Constantinople. The braiding varies by region: a three-strand plait is most common in mainland Greece.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze sliced for up to 1 month. Thaw at room temperature.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The dough can prove overnight in the fridge after shaping. Remove 45 minutes before baking and allow to come to room temperature.

glossaryTerms.definition: The dried, ground kernel of the St Lucie cherry stone, used in Greek, Lebanese, and Turkish baking. It gives tsoureki its distinctive almond-cherry-anise aroma. Buy the kernels whole and grind them in a small mortar just before use; pre-ground mahlab stales quickly and loses its fragrance within weeks of opening.

content.text: Tsoureki is a slow, patient dough. The enrichment (butter, eggs, and sugar) slows the yeast, and the braid needs a full second rise to achieve the right open crumb. Do not rush either stage. The characteristic strands come from a well-developed gluten network; knead the dough for a full 10 minutes.
and orange zest. Grind the mahlab kernels yourself if possible; the pre-ground powder sold in supermarkets has usually lost most of its fragrance.

Combine 500 g flour, 100 g sugar, 8 g salt, 7 g yeast, 1.5 tsp ground mahlab, the zest of 1 orange, and 1 tsp vanilla. Add 3 beaten eggs and 100 ml warm milk. Mix to a rough dough, then knead for 8 minutes until the dough is smooth. Add 120 g softened butter in four additions, kneading well after each. Continue kneading for 5 minutes more until the dough is smooth, shiny, and pulls away from the bowl cleanly.
Cover the dough and leave at room temperature until doubled, about 90 minutes. The enrichment slows the rise; a cool kitchen may take 2 hours.

Divide into three equal pieces, each about 230 g. Roll each into a strand roughly 50 cm long. Pinch the three strands together at one end and braid: right over centre, left over centre, repeat. Pinch the ends together and tuck underneath. Transfer to a parchment-lined tray. Cover and prove for 50 to 70 minutes until visibly puffy; the strands should look swollen and the braid should wobble gently when you shake the tray.

Heat the oven to 180 degrees C fan. Brush the braid with beaten egg, taking care not to press or deflate the rise. Scatter over 1 tbsp sesame seeds if using. Bake for 25 to 30 minutes until deep golden brown. The loaf should sound hollow when tapped on the base and register 90 to 92 degrees C on a probe thermometer. Cool on a rack for at least 20 minutes before slicing.

items.problem: Braid split open and spread sideways
items.solution: The dough was over-proofed and the gluten structure could not hold the shape during baking. The braid should be puffy but still hold its form when the tray is shaken, not quivering. Reduce the second prove time by 15 minutes.
The gluten was not developed enough. Enriched doughs need thorough kneading, particularly the stage after adding the butter. The dough should be smooth and elastic before the bulk ferment.
Pre-ground mahlab is often stale by the time it reaches the home baker. Buy whole kernels and grind them in a mortar just before use. The fresh-ground powder is significantly more fragrant.
